title: The Nervous System and Immune Function: Stress, Trauma, and Getting Sick
excerpt: Explore how stress and trauma impact your nervous system and immune function, influencing susceptibility to illness and recovery.
body:
Introduction: The Body’s Hidden Network

Many people notice that when they’re stressed or emotionally overwhelmed, they get sick more often or take longer to recover. This isn’t just coincidence. The nervous system and the immune system are deeply interconnected. Understanding how stress and trauma influence immune function is critical for anyone doing self-directed healing work or feeling stuck despite therapy.

Researchers like Bessel van der Kolk, Stephen Porges, and Gabor Maté have illuminated this link, revealing how trauma and chronic stress physically alter our bodies and shape illness vulnerability.

The Nervous System: More Than Fight or Flight

The nervous system isn’t just about reacting to immediate threats. It’s a complex regulatory system that manages everything from heart rate to digestion, immune surveillance to emotional regulation. Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory expanded our understanding by showing how the autonomic nervous system (ANS) has three hierarchical circuits:

  • Ventral Vagal: Supports social engagement and calm states.
  • Sympathetic: Activates fight-or-flight responses.
  • Dorsal Vagal: Governs shutdown or freeze reactions.

This hierarchy is essential because chronic stress or trauma can cause the nervous system to get stuck in defensive states, disrupting immune function.

Stress, Trauma, and Immune Dysregulation

When we experience stress or trauma, the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activates, releasing cortisol and other stress hormones. Acute stress can boost immune function temporarily, but chronic stress leads to immune dysregulation:

  • Suppressed immune responses: Reduced ability to fight infections.
  • Increased inflammation: Persistent low-grade inflammation linked to autoimmune diseases.
  • Altered immune cell distribution: Changes in natural killer cells and lymphocytes.

Bessel van der Kolk notes in The Body Keeps the Score that trauma survivors often experience chronic inflammation and immune dysfunction, contributing to chronic illness.

How the Nervous System Modulates Immunity

The nervous system communicates with the immune system through neural and biochemical pathways:

  • Vagus nerve: A key player that regulates inflammation via the cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway.
  • Sympathetic nervous system: Releases norepinephrine, influencing immune cell activity.
  • Cytokine signaling: Immune cells produce cytokines that affect brain function and vice versa.

Deb Dana highlights how vagal tone—the activity of the ventral vagal complex—helps maintain homeostasis, promoting both emotional regulation and immune resilience.

Trauma’s Somatic Imprint on Immunity

Trauma is stored in the body, as Pat Ogden’s sensorimotor psychotherapy research shows. This somatic imprint can dysregulate autonomic responses and thus immune function:

  • Hyperarousal: Persistent sympathetic activation leads to chronic inflammation.
  • Dissociation and shutdown: Dorsal vagal dominance can suppress immune responses, increasing infection risk.

Gabor Maté links early childhood trauma to compromised immune systems, explaining the biological embedding of adverse experiences.

The Role of Chronic Inflammation in Illness

Chronic inflammation is a common pathway linking trauma, stress, and disease. It’s associated with:

  • Autoimmune diseases like rheumatoid arthritis and lupus.
  • Cardiovascular disease.
  • Depression and anxiety disorders.
  • Neurodegenerative diseases.

Dan Siegel emphasizes that regulating the nervous system reduces inflammation and fosters neuroplasticity, which supports healing on multiple levels.

Somatic Healing and Nervous System Regulation to Support Immunity

Body-based approaches help recalibrate the nervous system and thus improve immune function. Techniques supported by research include:

  • Trauma-sensitive yoga: Reduces sympathetic arousal and inflammation (van der Kolk).
  • Somatic Experiencing: Peter Levine’s method to renegotiate trauma responses.
  • Polyvagal-informed practices: Deb Dana’s protocols for enhancing ventral vagal tone.
  • Mindful breathing and meditation: Shown to enhance vagal tone and lower inflammatory markers.

These interventions help move the nervous system out of chronic defense states, improving immune resilience.

Practical Tips for Supporting Your Nervous System and Immunity

  • Recognize your triggers: Awareness helps prevent chronic stress activation.
  • Engage in social connectedness: Positive social interactions boost ventral vagal tone.
  • Practice regular body-based self-regulation: Movement, breathwork, and grounding exercises.
  • Seek trauma-informed support: Therapists trained in somatic approaches can guide healing safely.
“The nervous system and immune system are in constant conversation, shaping how we experience health and illness.” – Bessel van der Kolk

Conclusion: Healing the System as a Whole

Getting sick is rarely a simple matter of germs alone. Stress and trauma exert powerful effects on the nervous system that ripple into immune function, making us more vulnerable to illness or slower to heal. The good news is that understanding this connection opens the door to self-directed healing that addresses root causes, not just symptoms. By regulating the nervous system through somatic work and nervous system-informed practices, you can support your immune health and overall resilience.

For those who have tried traditional therapy without full resolution, exploring the nervous system’s role in immunity offers a promising path forward—one grounded in science and lived experience.

References:

  • van der Kolk, B. (2015). The Body Keeps the Score. Viking.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory. Norton.
  • Maté, G. (2018). Scattered Minds. Vintage Canada.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice. North Atlantic Books.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Norton.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body. Norton.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind. Guilford Press.
  • Shapiro, F. (2017). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: How does chronic stress affect my immune system?
faqs.a: Chronic stress dysregulates the immune system by suppressing immune responses and increasing chronic inflammation, which can lead to greater illness susceptibility.

faqs.q: Why do trauma survivors often have health problems?
faqs.a: Trauma can cause persistent nervous system dysregulation, leading to chronic inflammation and immune dysfunction, which increase the risk of various physical and mental health issues.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapies improve immune function?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic therapies that regulate the nervous system, like Somatic Experiencing and trauma-sensitive yoga, can reduce inflammation and improve immune resilience.

faqs.q: What role does the vagus nerve play in immune health?
faqs.a: The vagus nerve helps regulate inflammation by transmitting calming signals from the brain to the body, promoting balanced immune responses.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma affects the body and offers insights into somatic healing approaches.

metaTitle: Nervous System, Stress, Trauma & Immune Function
metaDescription: Learn how stress and trauma impact your nervous system and immune health, and discover somatic tools to support healing and resilience.
